questions.q: Which is the most precise statement of Lenz's law?
options: The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the magnetic flux
The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the magnet
The induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux that produced it
The induced current always flows clockwise when viewed from the source
questions.explanation: The key word is 'change'. The induced current opposes the change in flux, not the flux and not the magnet. 'Opposes the flux' (A) is wrong when flux is decreasing — then the induced field reinforces the original flux to maintain it.

questions.q: What does the minus sign in Faraday's law \( \varepsilon = -N\dfrac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} \) represent?
options: That the EMF is always a negative number
Lenz's law — the direction of the induced EMF, which opposes the change
That energy is lost as heat in the coil
That flux can never increase
questions.explanation: The minus sign is Lenz's law written into the maths: it gives the direction (opposing the change). For any magnitude calculation you drop it — it carries direction only, never a number. Reporting a negative EMF in a magnitude answer loses marks.

questions.q: Lenz's law is described as 'conservation of energy in disguise'. If an induced current instead REINFORCED the change that produced it, what would result?
options: The induced current would simply be larger but harmless
A runaway feedback loop creating energy from nothing — a perpetual motion machine
The coil would overheat but energy would still be conserved
Nothing — both directions are equally valid
questions.explanation: A reinforcing current would accelerate the source, increasing the rate of change of flux, inducing a larger current and force, accelerating it more — a runaway that builds kinetic and electrical energy with no input. That perpetual motion machine violates conservation of energy, so it cannot happen.

questions.q: When you push a magnet into a coil, you must do mechanical work. Following the energy pathway ('Work, Watts, Warmth'), where does that energy ultimately go?
options: It is stored permanently as magnetic energy in the coil
It is destroyed by the opposing force
It becomes electrical energy of the induced current, then heat in the wire's resistance
It is returned to your hand as the magnet rebounds
questions.explanation: The coil opposes the motion, so you do work against it. That mechanical work becomes electrical energy (the induced current), which dissipates as heat via \( P = I^2R \). Every joule of induced electrical energy is paid for by a joule of mechanical work — energy is conserved.

questions.q: Using the 'Change, Oppose, Grip' procedure: the flux through a loop is INCREASING. Which way must the induced magnetic field inside the loop point?
options: In the same direction as the external field, to add to it
Opposite to the external field, to fight the increase
At right angles to the external field
It does not matter; flux direction is irrelevant
questions.explanation: To oppose an increase, the induced field points opposite to the external field, trying to stop the flux from rising. (If flux were decreasing, the induced field would instead point the same way as the original, to replace the lost flux.)

questions.q: A north pole approaches the face of a coil. By Lenz's law, the near face of the coil becomes a north pole. What is the effect on the magnet?
options: The coil attracts the magnet, speeding it up
The coil repels the magnet, opposing its approach
The coil has no effect on the magnet's motion
The magnet is deflected sideways
questions.explanation: A north pole on the coil's near face repels the approaching north pole, opposing the motion that increases the flux. This opposing force is exactly what conservation of energy demands — you must do work to push the magnet in.

questions.q: A flat metal ring sits in a field pointing into the page. You pull the ring out of the field. Which way does the induced current flow, viewed from the front?
options: Anticlockwise, to create a field out of the page
Clockwise, to create a field into the page that maintains the lost flux
It alternates direction rapidly
No current flows because the ring is solid
questions.explanation: The into-page flux is decreasing, so the induced current must create its own into-page field to replace the leaving flux. By the right-hand grip rule, an into-page field requires a clockwise current (viewed from the front). The current stops once the ring fully exits the field.

questions.q: A 50-turn coil of radius \( 0.10\,\mathrm{m} \), plane perpendicular to the field, has its field reduced from \( 0.40\,\mathrm{T} \) to \( 0 \) in \( 0.20\,\mathrm{s} \). What is the magnitude of the induced EMF? (\( A = \pi r^2 \))
How do Faraday's law and Lenz's law divide the work of describing electromagnetic induction?
options: Faraday gives the direction; Lenz gives the magnitude
Faraday gives the magnitude of the EMF; Lenz gives its direction
Both give only the magnitude
Faraday applies to coils; Lenz applies only to magnets
questions.explanation: Faraday tells you how big the induced EMF is (turns times rate of change of flux); Lenz tells you which way it points (opposing the change). Together they are the one law of induction, with Lenz appearing as the minus sign in Faraday's equation.

questions.q: A bar magnet is dropped north-pole-down toward a horizontal coil in a closed circuit. As it approaches, the magnet:
options: Accelerates faster than free fall, because the coil pulls it down
Falls more slowly than free fall, because the induced current opposes its motion
Falls at exactly the acceleration due to gravity
Stops completely above the coil
questions.explanation: The approaching magnet increases the flux; the induced current makes the coil's top face a north pole that repels the magnet, producing an upward force opposing the motion. The magnet decelerates and falls more slowly, with some gravitational PE going into electrical energy and heat.
